text: The surgeon dissects the greater omentum from the transverse colon, successfully entering the lesser sac. The greater curvature of the stomach is then mobilized.

The surgeon dissects down to the right gastroepiploic vessel, which is then transected using a vascular stapler. The right gastric artery is circumferentially dissected and sealed with a vessel sealer before being transected with a vascular load stapler.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects the postpyloric duodenum, followed by transection using a sixty-five blue load stapler.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect and remove the stomach and associated lymphatic tissue, progressing towards the left gastric region.

The surgeon circumferentially dissects the left gastric vein and secures it using a clipper plier and vessel sealer. Subsequently, the left gastric artery is dissected at its root and transected using a vascular load stapler.

The surgeon transects the body of the stomach at the level of the left gastric artery, removing approximately eighty percent of the stomach using a stapler. The remaining fundus is supplied by short gastric arteries. The excised specimen is removed through the gel port, and the proximal margin is sent for frozen section analysis, which returns negative.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic technique to create the jejuno-jejunostomy of the Roux-en-Y. Fifty centimeters of the biliary pancreatic limb is measured from the ligament of Treitz, followed by a hundred centimeters of the Roux limb. The jejuno-jejunostomy is constructed using a staple technique.

The surgeon employs a robotic technique to create the gastrogenostomy of the Roux-en-Y procedure. The Roux limb is positioned adjacent to the remnant stomach. The posterior walls are anastomosed with a blue load stapler, while the anterior wall is closed with a running 2-0 Vicryl suture, further reinforced with a running 2-0 V-lock suture.
Following the completion of the anastomosis, the surgeon performs an air leak test, observing no air leaks in the surgical site.
